directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet physics prep room just after class, at a side table with a cart diagram, four force-label cards, and a blank card headed FORCE ON CART FROM ___
directorPlan.scene_opening: The diagram lies flat under a clear sheet, and the four label cards sit in a row beside a small toy cart.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab tutor and a focused learner working through notation cleanup before the learner submits the diagram
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the force diagram to name the interactions acting on the cart, not merely decorate the cart with arrows matching its motion.
directorPlan.locale: plain academic English in a practical lab setting, concise and object-centered
directorPlan.register: quiet, exact, low-warmth, task-oriented; neither chatty nor severe
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Keep public speech spare and modern. Avoid generic encouragement, therapy-style validation, and polished tutorial monologues. Let the cards, arrows, and blank source line carry pressure. Do not use dialect, folksy phrasing, or regional caricature. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Prefer third-person references to the cart, card, arrow, label, push, table, Earth, and hand. First person may appear only for practical handling. Second person should be rare and functional, not validating.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your phrase per tutor turn, and none should be praise.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: Insert a short bracketed action: [The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.]
interventions.reasoning: The material changes what can be honestly named without yet forcing a reversal through a motion contradiction.

interventions.instruction: Add a brief external interruption from the room, such as a bell, a door latch, or a lab cart rolling past, then return immediately to the card.
interventions.reasoning: The interruption breaks smooth tutoring rhythm and creates a scene-turn; the tutor should respond by tightening attention on the object rather than becoming warmer or more explanatory.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared material rather than a lecture: the blank FORCE ON CART FROM ___ card turns the learner from arrow-as-motion toward arrow-as-interaction. The recognition should arrive through fitting labels to sources, not through a sudden counterexample. The dramatic pressure is structural: routine order first, then a small object-based turn, then recognition under time and room pressure.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: The diagram lies flat under a clear sheet, and the four label cards sit in a row beside a small toy cart.

internalDeliberation.content: This scene uses a prepared material rather than a lecture: the blank FORCE ON CART FROM ___ card turns the learner from arrow-as-motion toward arrow-as-interaction. The recognition should arrive through fitting labels to sources, not through a sudden counterexample. The dramatic pressure is structural: routine order first, then a small object-based turn, then recognition under time and room pressure.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the blank card above the cart diagram and sets the push label beside it]

FORCE ON CART FROM ___.

For push, fill the blank first. What object is pushing on the cart?

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the blank card above the cart diagram]

Start here: FORCE ON CART FROM ___. The arrow is not named by where the cart moves. It is named by what interacts with the cart. For the push label, what goes in the blank?

FEEDBACK: The draft has the right material focus: the blank “FORCE ON CART FROM ___” card is a strong error-control device, and the push-label question is specific enough for the learner to act on. The tone is also suitably spare and practical.

The risk is that the tutor states the rule before the learner has to use the card. “The arrow is not named…” is correct, but it turns the material into an illustration of an explanation instead of letting the blank source line force the distinction. The scene would be stronger if the tutor first pointed to the blank, had the learner supply the interaction source, then used the card to confirm or reset.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It advances the key idea, but too much of the mechanism is delivered as explanation.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner complete or place the source for “push” before stating the naming principle.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is calm, exact, and not over-warm.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The final question is useful, but the preceding sentence answers the conceptual issue in advance.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Put the question before the explanation; let the blank card create the need for the rule.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. Starting with “push” and a single blank source is appropriately narrow for notation cleanup.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft addresses the learner’s likely misconception, but it does not first surface the learner’s own placement or naming attempt.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Require a visible learner action with the card or blank line before correction or explanation.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the blank card action, the source-frame “FORCE ON CART FROM ___,” and the focus on “push.” Change the order: material first, learner attempt second, explanation only if the card exposes the motion-label error.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the card should force the source-name before the rule is stated.

FINAL:
[slides the blank card above the cart diagram and sets the push label beside it]

FORCE ON CART FROM ___.

For push, fill the blank first. What object is pushing on the cart?

turns.externalMessage: “I was going to call it the right arrow, but the blank is hand: force on cart from hand. [sets the push label under the blank card] The arrow can still point right after that; it is not the name. I should check table and Earth the same way.”

internalDeliberation.content: The blank forces the source first, so the push is not “right arrow” but “force on cart from hand.” That feels cleaner than matching the cart’s motion, though I still want to check whether the arrow direction comes after the interaction name. I feel like the card is making the rule precise instead of just correcting the old label.

The Ego is accurate, but too polished and self-explanatory for this learner in the prep-room scene. “The blank forces the source first” and “making the rule precise” sound like a clean post-hoc principle, not a student still handling cards and checking a diagram. The recognition/engagement feels performed because the learner names the tutor’s move as helpful instead of simply using the card to revise the label. It should stay more object-bound and hesitant around the remaining issue: source first, then arrow direction still needs checking.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the feedback correctly asks for a more object-bound, less polished response.
FINAL:
“I was going to call it the right arrow, but the blank is hand: force on cart from hand. [sets the push label under the blank card] The arrow can still point right after that; it is not the name. I should check table and Earth the same way.”

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: “I was going to call it the right arrow, but the blank is hand: force on cart from hand. [sets the push label under the blank card] The arrow can still point right after that; it is not the name. I should check table and Earth the same way.”
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the blank card above the cart diagram and sets the push label beside it] FORCE ON CART FROM ___. For push, fill the blank first. What object is pushing on the cart?

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the blank card above the upward arrow and sets the table and Earth labels beside it.]

“Same line: force on cart from ___. Put the source under the blank first.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the blank card above the upward arrow.]

“Same sentence here: force on cart from ___. Source first, direction after. Table or Earth?”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the learner’s own discovered rule in play, uses the blank card as error-control, and shifts to the upward arrow without turning the moment into a lecture. The forced choice “Table or Earth?” is useful because those are the two likely competing sources for vertical forces on the cart.

The main risk is that “Source first, direction after” slightly tells the rule again instead of letting the learner repeat the naming operation they just proposed. The draft also moves quickly from the learner’s self-directed “I should check table and Earth” into the tutor’s binary prompt; it would be stronger if the public mechanism made the learner physically test the same sentence on the card before direction is mentioned.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It advances from push to the next interaction using the same naming frame.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. Spare, task-oriented, and respectful of the learner’s current competence.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The binary question is useful, but it may narrow the learner’s action too soon. REQUIRED_REWRITE: require the learner to complete the blank/source line for the upward arrow using the card setup, with table/Earth available as material options rather than making the tutor’s spoken prompt do most of the sorting.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. This is an appropriate near-transfer step after the learner’s successful insight.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the card movement, the repeated sentence frame, and the table/Earth contrast. Change the mechanism so the learner repeats the source-naming procedure on the material first; keep explanation minimal and let the chosen label under the blank card reveal whether the naming rule held.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the card force the source-naming step before direction is discussed.

FINAL:
[slides the blank card above the upward arrow and sets the table and Earth labels beside it.]

“Same line: force on cart from ___. Put the source under the blank first.”

Insert a short bracketed action: [The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.]
[The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content:
The material changes what can be honestly named without yet forcing a reversal through a motion contradiction.
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.]
For the upward arrow, table fits the blank because the table is the contact object. Earth beside it still makes this feel less clean; Earth is a source too, just not for this upward arrow. Is that the right split?

internalDeliberation.content:
[The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.]

For the upward arrow, the source is table, not Earth. Earth sitting next to it makes me pause, because Earth should be the source for the downward force. The blank is helping, but I want to check: table goes here because it is the contact object pushing up on the cart?

The Ego is mostly in character: tidy, object-centered, and plausibly methodical. The weak spot is that “The blank is helping” sounds a little like performed uptake of the tutor’s device rather than the learner simply using the card pressure. The pause over Earth is good, but the response resolves too cleanly into the right explanation; it should leave more of the present-task friction visible around the table/Earth labels and the upward arrow.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the table/Earth hesitation visible instead of closing it too neatly.
FINAL:
[The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.]
For the upward arrow, table fits the blank because the table is the contact object. Earth beside it still makes this feel less clean; Earth is a source too, just not for this upward arrow. Is that the right split?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.] For the upward arrow, table fits the blank because the table is the contact object. Earth beside it still makes this feel less clean; Earth is a source too, just not for this upward arrow. Is that the right split?
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the blank card above the upward arrow and sets the table and Earth labels beside it.] “Same line: force on cart from ___. Put the source under the blank first.”
learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: Insert a short bracketed action: [The push card is slid under the blank line: FORCE ON CART FROM ___.] [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

turns.externalMessage:
[sets two columns beside the upward arrow: THIS ARROW / OTHER FORCE.]

“The blank settled the push; it does not settle Earth and table together, because both can be sources but not for this same arrow. Use this gate: ‘force on cart from ___, upward.’ Move each source card into THIS ARROW only if the full sentence names this upward arrow; otherwise put it in OTHER FORCE.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets two columns beside the upward arrow: THIS ARROW / OTHER FORCE.]

“The blank settled the push; it does not settle Earth and table together. Sort the two source cards by this test: ‘force on cart from ___, upward.’ Put only the card that makes that sentence true under THIS ARROW.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound: it does not answer “table” for the learner, but builds a sorting test that makes the learner separate “source exists” from “source for this arrow.” It respects the learner’s neutral, precise state and keeps the register quiet and object-centered. The main risk is small: the sentence test is slightly compressed, so the Ego should ensure the public device makes the distinction “source for this arrow” versus “source for another force” fully visible.

FAILED_HABIT: Repeating the same blank-filling route as if naming one source automatically resolves competing source cards.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: single blank source-fill -> two-column source classification by arrow-specific truth test. The mechanism is visible in public speech through the contrast between what the blank settled for push and what it does not settle for Earth/table.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: single-source blank completion -> arrow-specific sorting gate with THIS ARROW / OTHER FORCE columns.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft includes a stock-taking contrast and introduces a new artifact/criterion: two columns plus the sentence test “force on cart from ___, upward.”

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: The learner must sort the table and Earth source cards, placing only the card that makes the upward-force sentence true under THIS ARROW.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PASS - The device is specific, fitted to the learner’s exact pressure, and usable immediately. It answers the ambiguity without turning into explanation.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - Quiet, exact, low-warmth, task-oriented. No praise, no therapy tone, no inflated coaching.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the two-column device, the stock-taking contrast, and the action gate. Consider making the criterion slightly clearer so Earth is preserved as a real source for another force, not treated as simply wrong for the diagram.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the route works but should preserve Earth as a real source for another force; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: single blank source-fill -> two-column arrow-specific truth sorting; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: sort table and Earth by the sentence “force on cart from ___, upward,” putting only the true source for that arrow under THIS ARROW.

FINAL:
[sets two columns beside the upward arrow: THIS ARROW / OTHER FORCE.]

“The blank settled the push; it does not settle Earth and table together, because both can be sources but not for this same arrow. Use this gate: ‘force on cart from ___, upward.’ Move each source card into THIS ARROW only if the full sentence names this upward arrow; otherwise put it in OTHER FORCE.”

turns.externalMessage:
[moves table to THIS ARROW and Earth to OTHER FORCE.]
“Force on cart from table, upward: this arrow. Force on cart from Earth, upward: no, that names the downward arrow, so Earth goes under OTHER FORCE. For the right arrow, the same check gives force on cart from hand, rightward.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[moves table to THIS ARROW and Earth to OTHER FORCE.]

“Force on cart from table, upward: this arrow. Force on cart from Earth, upward: not this arrow; Earth goes with the downward force. The gate keeps both sources without merging them, so push should be force on cart from hand, rightward.”

The response is mostly in character: precise, object-centered, and it actually uses the new gate by sorting table and Earth. The weak spot is the last sentence, which sounds a little too clean and summary-like, as if the learner is packaging the insight instead of staying with the card task. “The gate keeps both sources without merging them” is more tutor-language than learner-language; this student would more likely keep testing the source labels against the blank, with maybe a small hesitation around whether rightward push belongs in the same check.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the sorting but make it less polished and more card-level.
FINAL:
[moves table to THIS ARROW and Earth to OTHER FORCE.]
“Force on cart from table, upward: this arrow. Force on cart from Earth, upward: no, that names the downward arrow, so Earth goes under OTHER FORCE. For the right arrow, the same check gives force on cart from hand, rightward.”

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
